summary: Spolsky explains why breaking the build in source control is a serious problem that can halt an entire team's productivity. He advocates for daily builds as a safeguard against unnoticed breakages. He recommends scheduling builds at lunchtime so developers can check in code beforehand and resolve any failures when they return. He shares the Excel team's practice of making the person who broke the build responsible for babysitting future builds as both punishment and education.
key_points: A single developer's forgotten checkin can block the entire team from working
Daily builds serve as an early warning system to catch breakages before they compound
Scheduling builds at lunchtime creates a natural workflow: checkin, break, then fix or continue
Rotating build babysitting duty through the person who broke it teaches everyone the build process
money_quotes: Breaking the build is so bad (and so common) that it helps to make daily builds, to insure that no breakage goes unnoticed.

They've added a new source file, and everything compiles fine on their machine, but they forgot to add the source file to the code repository. So they lock their machine and go home, oblivious and happy. But nobody else can work, so they have to go home too, unhappy.
On the Excel team we had a rule that whoever broke the build, as their "punishment", was responsible for babysitting the builds until someone else broke it.
This was a good incentive not to break the build, and a good way to rotate everyone through the build process so that everyone learned how it worked.
If the build failed, you fix it, but everybody can keep on working with the pre-build, unbroken version of the source.
key_insight: Daily builds with social accountability mechanisms like rotating build babysitting duty prevent broken builds from silently derailing team productivity.
